copyright_boundary: These are original procedural summaries. They identify public W3C sources but do not reproduce normative standard text.
usage_rule: Use a listed procedure only after fixing the target scope and opening its primary sources. This partial catalog adds procedures for four criteria only; it does not make an unlisted criterion executable or the profile complete.

applicability_steps: Inventory each user-presented non-text item in the fixed scope, including images, SVGs, icons, image controls, charts, audio, video, CAPTCHA, and decorative assets.
Classify the item's purpose from its visible context and operation: informative, functional or input, time-based media, test, sensory experience, CAPTCHA, or decorative/formatting/invisible.
Record the classification rationale and identify items whose purpose changes after interaction or with a changed state.
procedure_steps: For every applicable item, inspect the computed programmatic name, text alternative, adjacent equivalent text, or intentional mechanism that hides decoration from assistive technology.

Compare the inspected alternative with the information, action, state, or trend that the item conveys in the fixed target state; inspect a data table or prose alternative when a short label cannot carry equivalent chart or diagram information.

For image controls and input, exercise the permitted action and confirm that the accessible name describes the control purpose; record separately any related requirements that are outside this criterion.
For exceptions, verify the applicable exception conditions rather than treating the presence of an attribute as a result.
expected_results: Each applicable informative item has a text alternative that serves the equivalent purpose and conveys the needed information in its context.
Each applicable functional item has a programmatic name that describes its purpose, and decorative content is ignored by assistive technology rather than announced as meaningless content.
Items falling within an explicit exception have the descriptive identification or alternate modality appropriate to that exception.
cant_tell_when: The item's purpose, visible context, state-dependent meaning, or computed alternative cannot be observed in the permitted scope.
A complex chart, diagram, media item, CAPTCHA, or third-party asset cannot be compared with an equivalent text alternative or exception conditions.

pass: A chart has a concise label plus nearby text or a data table that communicates the same trend and values needed to use the chart.
A decorative icon is intentionally hidden from assistive technology while its adjacent text already conveys the only meaning.
fail: An informative chart is exposed only as a generic name such as 'image' or a filename, with no equivalent trend or data in text.
An image control has no programmatic name that describes what activating it does.
cant_tell: A screenshot shows an SVG label, but the reviewer cannot inspect its computed name or determine whether the visible data is fully available elsewhere in text.

procedures.ai_boundary: AI may inventory candidate non-text items or draft questions, but it must not record a profile outcome, human_verified mapping, or E2+ claim; an external human reviewer records any criterion result with target-specific evidence.

applicability_steps: Identify information, structure, and relationships conveyed through visual or auditory presentation in the fixed scope, including headings, lists, landmarks, tables, labels, required indicators, grouped fields, and state or status cues.
Identify relationships that are necessary to understand or operate the content when visual styling, position, grouping, color, or sound is unavailable.
Record any content or technology where relationships can only be checked through a linearized view or accessibility tree.
procedure_steps: Inspect the DOM, accessibility tree, or equivalent programmatic structure and compare it with the visible relationships in the same target state.

Check that headings, lists, tables, form labels, grouped controls, regions, and other applicable structures expose the relationships necessary for understanding or operation, or that an equivalent text statement is available near the content.
Review the content in a linearized or assistive-technology-facing representation when needed to determine whether the relationship survives presentation changes.
Record the exact visual relationship, programmatic or textual representation, and any unresolved boundary rather than inferring a result from tag presence alone.
expected_results: Information, structure, and relationships conveyed through presentation are programmatically determined or available in text for the scoped content.
Relationships needed to interpret tables, form fields, grouped controls, headings, lists, and regions remain available when presentation changes.
Semantic markup represents the actual content relationship rather than merely producing a visual effect.
cant_tell_when: The relevant relationship cannot be compared between the visual presentation and the DOM, accessibility tree, or linearized representation.
Dynamic or third-party content prevents the reviewer from determining whether the necessary relationship is programmatically determined or available in text.

pass: A data table exposes its row and column headers so that a data cell can be associated with the relevant headers.
A required-field indicator is explained in text and associated with the appropriate form fields rather than conveyed only by presentation.
fail: Visual headings are only bold paragraphs, with no programmatic heading or nearby text that conveys the section structure.
A table-like layout uses visual columns but does not expose the header-to-cell relationships needed to interpret its values.
cant_tell: A static screenshot suggests a grouped form or data table, but the reviewer cannot inspect its accessibility tree, linearized content, or source relationships.

procedures.ai_boundary: AI may inventory candidate relationships or draft questions, but it must not record a profile outcome, human_verified mapping, or E2+ claim; an external human reviewer records any criterion result with target-specific evidence.

applicability_steps: Fix the review scope and inventory every user-operable function available within it, including controls, composite widgets, drag or drawing interactions, and state-dependent actions.
Identify the permitted interactive states needed to expose each function and record any state that cannot be reached without a pointer or other unavailable input.

Identify any function whose underlying purpose depends on the path of the user's movement rather than only its endpoints, and require a specific rationale before treating that path-dependent function as an exception.
procedure_steps: Enter each required permitted state and exercise every applicable function through a keyboard interface only, without using a pointer or mouse emulation as the substitute test.
For each exercise, record the starting state, exact keys and sequence used, observed result, and any function or state that remains unreachable.

Check whether operation requires a particular timing for individual keystrokes; where a path-dependent exception is claimed, record the underlying function, required movement path, and why endpoints alone cannot perform it.

Record keyboard traps, character-key shortcuts, focus visibility, focus order, and related issues separately under their own criteria; use them here only when they also show that functionality cannot be operated through a keyboard interface.
expected_results: Every applicable function in the fixed scope and permitted interactive states can be operated through a keyboard interface without requiring specific timings for individual keystrokes.
Any excluded path-dependent function has a recorded rationale showing that its underlying purpose depends on the movement path and not merely the endpoints.
The review evidence identifies the keys used, the observed result, and any unreachable function rather than inferring operability from the presence of keyboard event handlers.
cant_tell_when: The interactive runtime, a required interactive state, or the keyboard path needed to exercise an applicable function cannot be reached in the permitted environment.
A claimed path-dependent exception cannot be exercised or its underlying movement-path dependency cannot be distinguished from endpoint-only operation.

pass: A custom menu can be opened, traversed, activated, and closed with documented keys in every permitted menu state without per-keystroke timing.

A freehand drawing function lacks keyboard operation, but the reviewer verifies and records that the function inherently depends on the path of the user's movement rather than only start and end points.
fail: A file picker opens with the keyboard, but one available file action can be completed only with a pointer.
A control recognizes a key only when it is pressed within a narrow timing window, even though the underlying function does not depend on movement path.
cant_tell: The reviewer can see a state-dependent editor control but cannot enter the required state or send the keyboard sequence in the permitted runtime.

procedures.ai_boundary: AI may inventory candidate functions, states, or keyboard paths and draft review questions, but it must not record a profile outcome, human_verified mapping, or E2+ claim; an external human reviewer records any criterion result with target-specific keyboard and manual evidence.

applicability_steps: Inventory native and custom user interface components in the fixed scope, including components that appear only after permitted interaction or in a changed state.

For each component, identify its visible purpose, expected role, every applicable user-settable value, state, or property, and the assistive-technology, accessibility API, or equivalent accessibility-interface control path that can request each setting.

Identify components whose DOM or accessibility-tree exposure, applicable programmatic-set control path, or change behavior cannot be inspected in the permitted environment, and keep status-message-only behavior under SC 4.1.3 outside this procedure.
procedure_steps: Before interaction, inspect each native and custom component in the DOM and accessibility tree and record its programmatic name, role, and current user-settable values, states, or properties.
Perform each permitted interaction, then inspect the DOM and accessibility tree after the change and compare the exposed name, role, value, state, and property with the component's visible behavior.

For every applicable user-settable value, state, or property, use assistive technology, an accessibility API client, or an equivalent accessibility interface to attempt to programmatically set a distinct permitted setting through that interface.

Before each programmatic-set attempt, record the current value, state, or property and the requested setting; record the resulting value, state, or property after the attempt, whether the requested setting took effect, and the programmatic and visible observations.
Separately observe whether each change is exposed and notified to user agents, including assistive technology, and record the prior exposure, changed exposure, and notification observed.
Record status-message-only behavior separately under SC 4.1.3; do not use a status message by itself as evidence for this component name, role, value, state, or property procedure.
expected_results: Each applicable component exposes a programmatically determinable name and role that match its purpose in the reviewed state.

Every applicable user-settable value, state, or property accepts the requested permitted setting through assistive technology, an accessibility API, or an equivalent accessibility interface and exposes the resulting setting.
Changes to applicable values, states, and properties are separately exposed and notified to user agents, including assistive technology.
Native and custom components preserve accurate programmatic exposure before and after each permitted interaction.
cant_tell_when: The component's accessibility-tree exposure, interactive behavior, or assistive-technology notification cannot be observed in the permitted environment.
No applicable assistive-technology, accessibility API, or equivalent accessibility-interface control path is available to issue and observe a programmatic set operation.
A required state or change cannot be triggered, so the reviewer cannot compare the component before and after interaction.

pass: An accessibility API set request programmatically sets a custom slider from 20 to 40, the resulting value is exposed as 40, and the change notification is announced to assistive technology.
A custom disclosure exposes its label and button role, accepts an assistive-technology request to set its expanded state, exposes the requested resulting state, and notifies the state change.
fail: A custom toggle's changed state is exposed and its notification is announced after pointer operation, but an accessibility API set request fails and the state cannot be programmatically set through the accessibility interface.
An icon-only custom control performs an action but has no programmatically determinable name describing its purpose.
cant_tell: A custom control's value is visible in the accessibility tree, but no permitted assistive-technology or accessibility API control path is available to issue and observe a set request.

procedures.ai_boundary: AI may inventory candidate components, compare captured DOM or accessibility-tree states, or draft review questions, but it must not record a profile outcome, human_verified mapping, or E2+ claim; an external human reviewer records any criterion result with target-specific browser and assistive-technology evidence.
